posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I really stumbled on this little find: Three bottles of 1999 Les Carmes Haut Brion for $29.99 each.

Taking a flyer was easy.

This wine delivers in boat-loads. Fully mature but could easily go longer. Great typicity with an alluring, soft Bordeaux perfume, musky, hint of game and wet leather. All red fruits, medium body, smoky, charcoal and dry earth. Ever so slight hint of citrus. A really elegant, appealing, accessible Bordeaux that does not try to be anything more than what it is. Extremely well balanced.
(91 pts.).

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked: Good find Robert. '99 is a mixed bag at lower levels I think but there are gems. One wonders if they make 'em like they used to. I wonder…

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Sounds great, like my kind of claret.

At some point, Rolland was brought in to this chateau, I’m pretty sure, and around the same time this became a Parker darling. I wonder if the 99 was before this happened?

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: I like to serve these with a nice steak, nearly raw, topped by a big pat of chamois butter.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: As good as it is today – it was even better 5 years earlier. I know it because I owned a case and have 2 bottles left. It has not lost the complexity but some richness. But I agree – still a fine wine.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Pat Martin:

Sounds great, like my kind of claret.

At some point, Rolland was brought in to this chateau, I’m pretty sure, and around the same time this became a Parker darling. I wonder if the 99 was before this happened?

I know Derenoncourt now consultants with this estate, but am not aware of Rolland consulting before that. Did some googling, but could not come up with anything. Now curious. I did not detect his heavy hand in this wine. If he consulted for the 1999, then kudos to him!

I thought you preferred the roast beef, tainted!

posts.name: Faryan Amir-Ghassemi
posts.cooked:
Cheers on the find RA. I think we’re coming around the bend on a lot of 2005 “petit” BDX, and of course vintages like 96, 98, 99, 00, 01 and soon 04,08 are in the wings as well.

The more other regions I focus on, the more I marvel at how Bordeaux almost singularly ages with grace across such a wide spectrum of quality.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked: I have had this recently and I thought it was stupendously good.

posts.name: Pat_Martin

Robert Alfert:
Pat Martin:

Sounds great, like my kind of claret.

At some point, Rolland was brought in to this chateau, I’m pretty sure, and around the same time this became a Parker darling. I wonder if the 99 was before this happened?

I know Derenoncourt now consultants with this estate, but am not aware of Rolland consulting before that. Did some googling, but could not come up with anything. Now curious. I did not detect his heavy hand in this wine. If he consulted for the 1999, then kudos to him!

Oops, I was thinking of Larrivet-Haut-Brion, that’s where Rolland consults. I own a bottle or two of the 2005 Carmes Haut Brion, and now I’m (more) glad that I do!

That’s what I get for posting before my morning coffee.

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Larrivet is a great value pessac

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Nice to hear it’s still doing well. And that’s a great price. I paid $28 in 2001. The three bottles I had between 03 and 09 all showed nicely, though none was spectacular.

I visited there in June 2001 and the owner met us at the front gate with a napsack over his shoulder. The cellar was simple and not that clean. It was refreshing to see a Bordeaux property that wasn’t all spit and polish, and the wines seemed quite honest. I think the production is quite small, so you don’t see them often in the US.

It was originally a Carmelite property – hence the name – and then it was seized and turned into a park after the revolution.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Marc Frontario:

Liar rivet is a great value pessac

I liked the (presumed) iphone change of spelling for Larrivet to “Liar rivet”.

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: indeed!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Ah, enjoying a very large glass that remained from last night.

Still strutting its stuff, picked up some weight and a little salinity has come to the fore.

I’d be remiss if I did not admit the presence of a lovely little hint of brett, showing some elegant rusticity that I absolutely adore in a red wine.

This wine, this year, is dead-center in my wheelhouse.

BTW, what a pretty label on this wine!

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
I could swear I paid more on release, and still have been letting them slumber. One of my favorite Graves. I chatted with the owners a few years ago at a UGC tasting, surprisingly few people were stopping in at their table. I mentioned them to them that I had self imported some 82, long gone now, and they were very surprised to hear that. I assumed the chateaux had lots of the 82 in their library, but apparently that was not the case, very few have the luxury/ruthlessless of LLC and first growths to keep their own wine back for decades.

The wines of the turn of the century era were much better than that time. They really make such balanced wines.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
I liked the '95 version.
Almost like a Right Bank in blend with supposedly 40% Cab Franc. I’m blanking on any other Left Bank wines that contain as much Cab Franc.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: wow. i had not realized that. that’s a figeac type level.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
R Cabrera:

I liked the '95 version.
Almost like a Right Bank in blend with supposedly 40% Cab Franc. I’m blanking on any other Left Bank wines that contain as much Cab Franc.

The Cab Franc is pronounced in this wine, has more of a red fruit profile with good acid. The percentage of CF explains why I like this wine so much. My favorite St Ems, and the only ones that I will buy these days, have high CF content (although most are now out of my pay grade).

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Stop drinking this damn wine without me…u know this is right in my wheelhouse u dirty dog…I think u have one more?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Dude, we have so much in cue before this daily drinker!!! We have tons of Loire CF!

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: Robert - if you like 99’s, you might want to take a look at the 99 Chambrun from K&L for $20. I paid way more on release, and even though mine are long gone, it was a really nice right bank. I suspect it has aged better than its pedigree suggests – I found a bottle of 98 in my stash last year by accident and it was still vibrantly good. My feel is that it ages even better than Fleur de Bouard, the other strong player in Lalande de Pomerol. I’d have bought some from K&L except that I had lots of the 98-01 series when I was younger, and as that stray bottle shows, I’m not even sure they’re all gone…

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Thanks Arv. I will grab some. I really loved the '99 La Fleur de Bouard, bought and went through 18 bottles. It was cheap like a house wine and damn good. Not loving more recent versions from this estate.

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
I had this superb Carmes Haut-Brion 1999 last friday.
Full report of a verticale tasting (at the domain) to follow (2014/1915, 22 vintages).
Love the house, love the vintage (for the most part) and love love love the price! Well done, counselor

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Neal.Mollen:

Love the house, love the vintage (for the most part) and love love love the price! Well done, counselor

Thank you, sir. I bit on some 2014 as well, price was too good to pass up. Have heard of some stylistic change, but am hoping that the higher percentage of cab franc keeps this wine more in my wheelhouse even it if (allegedly) is trending new world.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: Loved the 2012. But I haven’t had the older vintages to compare.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Agreed, a very nice estate. I would love to come across some older vintages.

Derenoncourt now consults, as others have observed, and the new director is ex-Chapoutier.

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
William,

Stéphane Derenoncourt attented this tasting …
Guillaume Pouthier and Guillaume Deschepper are ex-Chapoutier.
Cabernet-franc and “vendange entière”, for this urban vineyard.

2014 and 2012 are both excellent.

We ended with a surprising 1915 (magnum).

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Laurent, can you offer some intell on the style (better if you can contrast to the style of prior owners and winemakers)? In reading more, notably from Leve’s website, it seems as if this winery has moved into a more international style, lots of notes of dark, soft/lush, new oak, etc., with 14% alcohol in 2014.

Thanks!

posts.name: Laurent Gibet
posts.cooked:
Jeff Leve attended the tasting as well … I don’t know if he will publish something about this event at the domain.

I’ll do my best to further answer your question, Albert.

I can say that 2014 and 2012 are ripe and fresh, thin, very tasty.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
I’m picking up a 2012 this wknd. I was just thinking how long its been since I’ve had one of these, but they also feel like the kind of thing one should share with other enthusiasts.

I’d be interested in seeing the vertical notes. Presumably only the chateau was able to pull that together?
